segments.purgatorio-29-g31: E (and) is the simple coordinating conjunction linking this clause to what precedes. Una melodia dolce is an indefinite NP with the adjective dolce placed after the noun (a poetic/neutral word order in Dante). Correva is the imperfect (third-person singular) of correre used figuratively as ‘ran/flowed’ to describe a continuing sound. Per + l’ contracts the preposition and article (per + il → per l’) and aere is an archaic spelling of aria ‘air/atmosphere’; luminoso modifies it.
segments.purgatorio-29-g32: Onde here functions as a connective adverb meaning ‘from which/whereby’ (pointing back to the melody) and introduces the result clause. Buon zelo (‘good zeal’) is a noun phrase: buon is qualifying adjective before the noun. Mi fé riprender is a causative construction: fé (past of fare, ‘made/caused’) plus the infinitive riprender ‘to reproach/reprimand’; mi is the indirect object ‘me’. L’ardimento d’Eva is the object of riprender — the daring of Eve.
segments.purgatorio-29-g33: Che is a relative pronoun (‘who/which’) introducing a clause that further describes Eve. Là dove is an adverbial locution ‘there where’. Ubidia is an archaic form of ubidire/ubidire (third-person singular present in Dante’s orthography) meaning ‘is/are obedient / obey’; it is used here in a descriptive present. La terra e ’l cielo names the spheres ('earth and heaven') connected by e.
segments.purgatorio-29-g34: Femmina is an appositive noun—Dante’s vocative/appositive use to mark ‘woman’ or ‘female’ (here applied to Eve). It stands alone for emphasis and restates the identity of the antecedent.
segments.purgatorio-29-g35: Sola e pur testé formata is a compact adjectival phrase: sola ‘alone’; e pur ‘and yet/still’ (pur = tuttavia); testé (poetic/archaic) = ‘just now/newly’. Formata is a past participle used adjectivally (‘formed/created’), so the phrase means ‘alone and yet just now formed.’
segments.purgatorio-29-g36: Non sofferse di star is a past tense clause: sofferse is the passato (or Dantean past form) of soffrire meaning ‘endure/permit’; with non it means ‘did not endure/permit’. Di + infinitive (star = stare ‘to be/remain’) expresses the action she would not tolerate. Sotto alcun velo = ‘under any veil’, where alcun has the negative sense ‘any’ after the negation.
segments.purgatorio-29-g37: Sotto ’l qual = ‘under which’ (relative phrase referring to the veil just mentioned). Se introduces the conditional clause ‘if’; divota fosse stata is a past counterfactual: divota ‘devout/pious’ as predicate adjective, fosse (imperfect subjunctive of essere) + stata (past participle) together form the pluperfect/subjunctive sense ‘had been (devout)’ — i.e. a contrary-to-fact condition.
segments.purgatorio-29-g38: Avrei … sentite is the conditional perfect construction: avrei (conditional of avere, here an auxiliary) + past participle sentite meaning ‘I would have perceived/heard/experienced’. Quelle ineffabili delizie is the direct object ‘those ineffable delights’. Prima e più lunga fïata modifies the verb phrase: prima = ‘earlier/before’, più lunga fïata = ‘for a longer time’ (fïata = a time/space of time in Dante).
segments.purgatorio-29-g39: Mentr' is a poetic elision of mentre (while). io m'andava combines the subject io and the clitic mi + imperfect andava (andare, imperfect) meaning 'I was going' or 'I went along' — the imperfect marks a continuous past action. tra tante primizie de l’etterno piacer is a prepositional phrase ('among so many first-fruits of the eternal pleasure'); note de l’ is an older form of dell' (of the). tutto sospeso is a past-participial phrase used adjectivally ('wholly suspended'), with tutto functioning as an intensifier modifying sospeso.
segments.purgatorio-29-g40: e links this clause to the previous one. disïoso (the diaeresis shows the syllabic break dis-i-oso) is an adjective meaning 'desirous' and refers back to the speaker; ancora = 'still'. a più letizie uses the preposition a with più ('more') and the plural noun letizie ('joys, delights') — the whole phrase means 'for/unto greater delights', i.e. 'longing for still more joys.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g41: dinanzi a noi is a simple prepositional locution meaning 'before us' or 'in front of us'; dinanzi is followed by the preposition a and the pronoun noi.
segments.purgatorio-29-g42: tal quale is a fixed simile formula meaning 'such as' or 'just like'. un foco acceso is a noun phrase 'a kindled/blazing fire'; acceso is a past participle used adjectivally ('lit/ignited'). The sequence introduces a comparison: 'before us, such as a kindled fire.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g43: ci si fé is an impersonal/reflexive construction with the clitic ci ('for us/to us') plus si and the past tense fé (old orthography of fece, from fare) meaning 'was made/became (for us)'. The subject l'aere follows the verb (inverted order): 'the air (l'aere) made itself / presented itself to us beneath the green branches.' sotto i verdi rami is the locative 'under the green boughs.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g44: e connects this final clause. 'Il dolce suon' = 'the sweet sound' (dolce adjectival). per canti literally 'by/through songs' — i.e. coming from the singing. era già inteso uses era (imperfect of essere used here as a past-state auxiliary) + inteso (past participle of intendere) to mean 'was already heard' or 'had already been perceived'; Dante frequently uses this periphrasis to indicate a prior/perceived event.
segments.purgatorio-29-g45: O is the vocative interjection 'O' (address). sacrosante is an emphatic adjective 'sacrosanct, most holy' modifying Vergini; Vergini is vocative plural ('Virgins'), directly addressing them (the Virgin Mary and other saintly virgins).
segments.purgatorio-29-g46: Here se is the conjunction 'if' (an elliptical conditional: 'if [I have suffered] ...') and fami is an archaic form meaning 'fame/fame' (hunger) — an older spelling and usage. The phrase is elliptical: 'if hunger ...' meaning 'if I have suffered hunger'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g47: freddi o vigilie mai per voi soffersi lists the hardships: freddi 'colds' (cold, chill) or vigilie 'vigils' (nights kept awake). mai = 'ever'. per voi = 'for you' (indicating motive). soffersi is an older perfect form of soffrire (first person: 'I have suffered' or 'I suffered'), used with the omitted subject io — 'if I have ever endured cold or vigils for you.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g48: cagion mi sprona = 'reason/this cause spurs me' (cagion nominative); mi is the object clitic 'me' (urges me). sprona is present indicative 'spurs/urges'. ch’io introduces a subordinate clause with subjunctive: mercé vi chiami uses mercé ('mercy') as the object notion, vi = clitic 'you' (to you), and chiami is present subjunctive of chiamare ('that I call/invoke'), required here by the volitive/urging sense of sprona.
segments.purgatorio-29-g49: Or = 'now'. convien is an impersonal present ('it is necessary' / 'it is fitting'). che introduces a subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive. Elicona (Helicon, the mountain of the Muses) is the subject of the subjunctive verb that follows; per me = 'for me'. versi here is used as a verb in the subjunctive ('compose verse' / 'put into verse for me'), so it is the present subjunctive form demanded by convien che (Dante's verb form/orthography is archaic/poetic).
segments.purgatorio-29-g50: e links the next petition. Uranìe (the Uranias, the Muses) m’aiuti is a subjunctive wish/optative 'may the Uranias help me' (aiuti is present subjunctive; m' = mi 'me' object). col suo coro = 'with her/their chorus' (suo refers to the Muse(s) as source of song). forti cose a pensar mettere in versi is a compact infinitival chain: forti cose = 'weighty/strong matters'; a pensar ('to be thought' / 'hard to think') qualifies those things as difficult to conceive; mettere in versi = 'to put into verse' (mettere + in versi). The construction expresses: 'help me with their chorus to put weighty things that are hard to think into verse.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g1: Cantando is an Italian gerund (present participle) meaning "singing" and modifies the (implicit) subject; in poetry the gerund often begins a clause. come donna innamorata is a simile: come = "like/as," donna = "lady/woman," and innamorata is an adjective (past participle used adjectivally) meaning "in love." The phrase as a whole describes the manner in which she sang.
segments.purgatorio-29-g2: continüò is the passato remoto (past tense) meaning "she continued" (the diaeresis marks the pronunciation). col is the contraction of con + il = "with the," di = "of," sue is the feminine possessive "her," and parole is the plural noun "words/speech." Col fin di sue parole therefore means "with/to the end of her words," i.e. she carried on to finish her speech.
segments.purgatorio-29-g3: This segment is a Latin quotation. Beati is Latin nominative plural "blessed"; quorum is a Latin relative pronoun (genitive plural) meaning "whose"; tecta is a perfect passive participle/predicate adjective meaning "covered," agreeing with peccata; sunt is Latin present indicative "are"; peccata is neuter plural "sins." The whole line reads in sense: "Blessed are they whose sins are covered."
segments.purgatorio-29-g4: This segment contains only the closing punctuation (a closing quotation mark/apostrophe and period) that ends the direct speech; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-29-g5: E = "and." come = "like/as," introducing a simile. ninfe = "nymphs" (classical/mythical women). che = relative pronoun "who/which." si + givan is a poetic/archaic past-form construction meaning "they went" or "they were going" (si is the reflexive/pronominal particle attached to the verb). sole here qualifies the nymphs as "alone" (poetic use of 'solo/sole'). per = "through," le = feminine plural article "the," salvatiche = adjective "wild/untamed," and ombre = "shades/shadows." The clause compares the women's movement to nymphs moving alone through wild shadowy places.
segments.purgatorio-29-g6: disïando is a gerund/participle meaning "desiring/longing." qual is used distributively here ("some/one or another") and di introduces the infinitive that follows; veder is the infinitive "to see." Together qual di veder means roughly "some (of them) desiring to see." The structure shows two opposed wishes among the nymphs.
segments.purgatorio-29-g7: This clause mirrors the previous: qual = "some," di introduces the infinitive fuggir = "to flee/avoid," lo = masculine singular definite article "the," and sole = "sun." Qual di fuggir lo sole therefore means "some desiring to flee the sun." The pair qual...qual contrasts the differing motives.
segments.purgatorio-29-g8: allor = "then/thereupon." si mosse is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of muoversi, reflexive: "she/they moved" or "she moved herself" (here: the woman moved). contra ’l fiume = "toward the river," with contra used in the sense "toward/against" and ’l = contraction of il "the." The clause reports the woman's action in the narrative past.
segments.purgatorio-29-g9: andando is the gerund "going" describing accompanying movement. su per la riva is a prepositional phrase meaning "up along the bank/shore" (su per = "up along"; la riva = "the bank/shore"). The gerund + prepositional phrase shows manner/direction of her motion.
segments.purgatorio-29-g10: e io pari di lei = "and I like her / I equal her." io = "I." pari is an adjective meaning "equal/like," used predicatively to express that the speaker kept pace with or matched her. di lei is a (poetic/archaic) prepositional phrase meaning "to/with her" (modern Italian would use a or a pari di lei). The clause states the narrator's relation to her movement.
segments.purgatorio-29-g11: picciol passo = "with a small/short step" (picciol = "small/short," passo = "step/pace"). con = "with." picciol seguitando uses seguitando (gerund of seguitare) meaning "following/keeping on"; together the phrase means "following with small steps" or "keeping pace with small steps." The gerund seguitando indicates attendant action (the narrator following her).
segments.purgatorio-29-g12: Non eran is the imperfect tense "there were not" (non + erano). cento = "a hundred" (not listed among the provided words). tra = "between/among." suoi passi = "her steps" (suoi = possessive "her/their," passi = "steps"). e miei = "and mine" (miei = possessive "my/mine"). The clause quantifies the short distance between their footsteps.
segments.purgatorio-29-g13: quando = "when." le ripe = "the banks/shores" (ripe = plural of ripa). igualmente = "equally/uniformly." dier volta is an archaic/poetic expression: dier is a past-form (from dare in an idiomatic sense) and volta = "turn," so together it means "turned" or "gave a turn" (i.e. the banks turned equally). The clause sets the time when the banks made a corresponding bend.
segments.purgatorio-29-g14: per modo ch’ = "in such a way that" (per modo = "by/through a manner," ch’ = che = "that"). a levante = "to the east/king of the rising," where levante is a noun meaning "the east/rising (sun)." mi rendei is passato remoto first person singular of rendersi/ rendere used reflexively: "I turned (myself)/I found myself" or "I turned toward the east." The whole gives the result: by virtue of that turn I oriented toward the east.
segments.purgatorio-29-g15: Né ancor fu così nostra via molta is a compact, literary clause: 'Né ancor' = 'nor yet' (negative connective + temporal adverb) precedes the verb; 'fu' is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of essere meaning 'was/had been' in a narrative style; 'nostra via' (our path/journey) is postverbal for poetic word order; 'molta' qualifies via as 'great/far' ('much' in extent). The whole means that their journey had not yet gone so far.
segments.purgatorio-29-g16: quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'; 'la donna' = 'the woman'; 'tutta' is an intensifier here meaning 'entirely/wholly'; 'a me' is the prepositional object 'to me'; 'si' is the reflexive pronoun and combines with 'torse' (passato remoto of torcere) to form 'si torse' = 'turned herself' or 'turned toward me'. Word order 'tutta a me si torse' is emphatic and poetic.
segments.purgatorio-29-g17: dicendo is the present gerund of dire ('saying') and links the act of turning to the reported speech that follows; it indicates simultaneous action and introduces the direct quotation.
segments.purgatorio-29-g18: Frate mio is a vocative address. Frate here is used as 'brother' (a form of address), and mio is the possessive 'my' placed after the noun for emphatic, familiar address.
segments.purgatorio-29-g19: guarda e ascolta are two second-person-singular imperatives ('look' and 'listen') joined by e ('and'); they are commands addressed to the addressee just named in the vocative.
segments.purgatorio-29-g20: Ed ecco is an exclamatory opening ('and behold'); un lustro here uses lustro as a luminous 'gleam/flash' (not the five-year period); sùbito = 'suddenly'; trascorse is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of trascorrere used transitively/intransitively to mean 'passed/ran by'; da tutte parti = 'from all sides'; per la gran foresta = 'through the great forest' (gran = poetic apocope of grande).
segments.purgatorio-29-g21: tal che introduces a result clause ('such that'); di balenar uses the infinitive balenar substantively ('the flashing' / 'the flash') and functions as the cause; mi mise in forse is idiomatic: mise (passato remoto of mettere) + 'mi' (= 'me') + 'in forse' = 'put me in doubt' or 'made me hesitate'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g22: Ma perché begins a causal/explanatory clause ('but because'); the apostrophized l (ʼl) is the article 'il' before balenar; balenar again is the infinitive used as a substantive 'the flash' or 'the flashing'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g23: come vien = 'as it comes' or 'when it appears'; come is a comparative/temporal 'as', and vien is present indicative (venire) used generically in narrative to mean 'it comes/appears'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g24: resta is the present indicative (restare) 'remains' — the clause states that the flash, once it appears, does not immediately vanish but stays.
segments.purgatorio-29-g25: e quel = 'and that (one)'; quel is the demonstrative 'that' referring back to a light or gleam mentioned, linking the two contrasted behaviors.
segments.purgatorio-29-g26: durando is the present gerund of durare ('lasting/continuing') describing the continuing action of 'that one' — 'that one, while continuing/enduring...'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g27: più e più splendeva is an emphatic repetition 'more and more' (più e più) with splendeva the imperfect of splendere 'shone/kept shining', showing progressive increase in brightness.
segments.purgatorio-29-g28: nel mio pensier dicea literally 'in my thought I was saying'; nel = in + article, mio = 'my', pensier = 'thought' (poetic form), and dicea is the imperfect of dire ('I was saying' / 'I kept saying'), marking an internal reflection.
segments.purgatorio-29-g29: ‘Che cosa è questa? is a direct question in thought: 'Che cosa' = 'what thing/what is', è = present indicative of essere 'is', questa = 'this' (demonstrative).
segments.purgatorio-29-g30: This segment contains only the closing punctuation of the quotation (the closing apostrophe/quote and the period); there are no lexical content words to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-29-g65: Subject + clitic + verb in past historic: 'La donna' (the lady) is the grammatical subject placed before the verb; 'mi' is the clitic direct object pronoun placed before the finite verb; 'sgridò' is the passato remoto (third person singular) of sgridare, giving a completed past action. The colon introduces the reported speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-29-g66: Direct question introduced by 'Perché' (why). 'Pur' is an adverb meaning 'still'/'even'; 'ardi' is second-person singular present (poetic/archaic) of ardere ('you burn' / 'you are burning'), used here to address the narrator. 'Sì' is an intensifier ('so'); 'ne' is a prepositional particle meaning 'in' or 'within' when combined with an abstract noun; 'l’affetto' = 'the feeling/affection' (note the elided article l'); 'de' is the archaic form of 'di' (of), and 'le vive luci' = 'the living lights' (vive = alive/vivid, luci = lights). Word order and archaic particles reflect Dante's poetic diction.
segments.purgatorio-29-g67: 'E' links the clauses ('and'). 'Ciò che' = 'that which' (demonstrative + relative). 'Vien' is third-person singular present of venire ('comes'); 'di retro a lor' = 'from behind them' (di = from; retro = behind; a lor = to/at them/them); 'non guardi' is a prohibitive/question form addressed to 'tu' — grammatically it can be read as present subjunctive or an imperative-like present form meaning 'do not look' / 'do you not look?'. The clause is a direct question admonishing the addressee not to look at what comes from behind the lights.
segments.purgatorio-29-g68: A single punctuation mark: a full stop (period) appearing alone as a verse line. It serves as the end of the preceding sentence/line and carries no lexical items.
segments.purgatorio-29-g69: 'Genti' (people) is the noun head; 'vid' is the apocopated form of 'vidi' (I saw), with the verb placed before the subject for poetic effect ('vid' io = vidi io). 'Allor' is the archaic form of 'allora' (then). The inversion (verb before subject) is common in Dante for metrical and rhetorical effect.
segments.purgatorio-29-g70: 'Come' introduces a comparison/apposition 'as'; 'a lor' is the dative-like phrase 'to them/their' (archaic 'lor' = modern 'loro'); 'duci' is nominative plural 'leaders' and functions as a role-appositive to the people seen — 'people, as leaders to them' (i.e. acting as leaders relative to those previously mentioned).
segments.purgatorio-29-g71: 'Venire' is the infinitive used as the object/complement of the perception verb 'vidi' ('I saw ... come'), a common poetic/narrative construction (I saw people come). 'Appresso' is an adverb meaning 'close after/behind'; it modifies 'venire' ('come up behind').
segments.purgatorio-29-g72: 'Vestite' is the past participle (feminine plural) of vestire used adjectivally and agreeing with 'genti' (dressed); 'di bianco' means 'in white' (di + color), so the whole phrase describes the people's clothing. The participle phrase follows the infinitive cluster describing the people seen.
segments.purgatorio-29-g73: 'E' links the final comment; 'tal candor' = 'such whiteness/brightness' (tal = such, candor = whiteness/brightness). 'Di qua' = 'on this side' or 'here on this side'; 'già mai non fuci' is an archaic negative past construction meaning 'never before was' or 'had never been' (note the emphatic 'già mai non' = 'never, ever before'). The verb form 'fuci' is an older/poetic form; the clause states that such whiteness had not formerly appeared here.
segments.purgatorio-29-g74: Elided article + noun subject: 'L’acqua' (the water). 'Imprendëa' is an imperfect tense (third singular) of the archaic/poetic verb 'imprendere' with an embedded diaeresis marking the vowel—here meaning 'began to seize/press upon' or 'took hold.' 'Dal sinistro fianco' is a prepositional phrase ('from/on the left flank') indicating where the action occurs.
segments.purgatorio-29-g75: 'E' links the clauses. 'Rendea' is the imperfect of rendere ('render/return/show'), with the clitic pronoun 'me' placed before the verb; here it means roughly 'gave/returned to me' or 'showed me.' 'La mia sinistra costa' is the feminine object phrase 'my left bank/side' (costa used as shore/side in context). The imperfect gives an ongoing or repeated action in the narrative.
segments.purgatorio-29-g76: 'S’io' is the poetic contraction of 'se io' (if I). 'Riguardava' is the imperfect tense of riguardare ('I looked at' / 'I turned my gaze toward'), used here in the protasis describing the condition under which the next image holds. 'In lei' = 'in it/in her' (referring to the water); the clause means 'if I looked into it.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g77: 'Come' = 'like/as'; 'specchio' = 'a mirror' (here a simile: the water acted like a mirror); 'anco' is an archaic form of 'anche' meaning 'also'/'as well'. The phrase completes the simile: if he looked into the water, it reflected him like a mirror too.
segments.purgatorio-29-g78: Quand' is a contracted form of quando = 'when'. da la mia riva ('from my bank/shore') specifies the place from which the action is done. ebbi is the passato remoto of avere used here to mean 'I took' or 'I had' (i.e. 'I took up' a position). tal posta = 'such a position/station' — posto/posta here means a standing-place or position.
segments.purgatorio-29-g79: che introduces a relative/result clause referring back to the position; solo = 'only'. il fiume is the subject; mi facea distante uses facea (imperfect of fare) plus the adjective distante, meaning 'made me distant' or 'separated me' (i.e. only the river stood between us). Word order is subject (il fiume) + verb + complement (mi distante).
segments.purgatorio-29-g80: per + infinitive (per veder) = 'in order to see'. meglio is comparative/adverb 'better'. ai passi ('at/to the steps/passes') indicates what he wanted to see more clearly. diedi sosta is passato remoto of dare + noun 'sosta' = 'I gave a stop' → 'I stopped'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g81: e = 'and'. vidi is passato remoto 'I saw'. le fiammelle is the direct object ('the little flames/sparks'). andar is the infinitive used with vidi in the classic 'see X do Y' construction: 'I saw them go'. davante is an adverb, 'ahead/in front'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g82: lasciando is a present participle 'leaving' that modifies the subject (the fiammelle). dietro a sé = 'behind themselves' (i.e. behind them). l’aere dipinto: l' aere ('the air/sky') + dipinto (past participle used adjectivally) = 'the air painted' or 'the sky painted' (i.e. colored like a painting).
segments.purgatorio-29-g83: e = 'and'. di tratti pennelli is a compressed, poetic phrase: tratti = 'strokes' and pennelli = 'brushes', so 'like brushstrokes'. avean is imperfect of avere (avevano) = 'they had'. sembiante = 'appearance' or 'resemblance' — together: 'and they had the appearance of brushstrokes.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g84: sì che = 'so that' (introducing the result). lì sopra = 'there above' or 'up there'. rimanea (rimaneva) is imperfect 'remained/was left'. distinto = 'distinct/clearly separated'. di sette liste places the counted item after the adjective in poetic inversion: 'distinct of seven bands' = 'seven distinct bands remained above.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g85: tutte 'all' (agreeing with liste). in quei colori 'in those colors'. onde here is a relative/adverbial 'with which' or 'of which' (poetic usage). fa (fare) = 'makes/forms' (present tense used poetically) and the clause that follows names two poetic images: l’arco il Sole = 'the Sun makes the arch' (i.e. the rainbow) and Delia il cinto = 'and Delia makes the girdle' (Delia = epithet of Diana/the Moon), so 'the Sun makes the arch and Delia the girdle.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g86: Questi = 'these/those' (demonstrative). ostendali is a poetic/rare form meaning 'things shown/displayed' (the reading or catalogue of sights); in dietro = 'behind'. eran = eran (imperfect of essere) 'were'. maggiori = plural comparative 'greater/larger' (here 'too large for'). che la mia vista = 'than my sight' — the clause omits an implied verb ('could take in' / 'could discern'), a common elliptical construction meaning 'larger than my eyes could manage.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g87: e = simple conjunction 'and', linking the two related statements.
segments.purgatorio-29-g88: quanto a mio avviso is a formulaic phrase 'as to my opinion' or 'in my judgment' — quanto a = 'as for/regarding', mio avviso = 'my view/opinion'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g89: diece passi = 'ten paces' (diece = archaic form of dieci). distavan is imperfect of distare = 'they were distant/they lay apart'. quei di fori = 'those from outside' or 'those outside' (quei = those; di fori = 'from outside' / 'outside ones') — word order places the distance before the subject: 'those outside were ten paces away.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g90: Sotto = 'under' or 'beneath'. così bel ciel = 'so beautiful a sky' (così + adjective + noun). com' = contraction of 'come' = 'as'. io diviso uses diviso (past participle of dividere) with io as subject — poetically rendered 'as I stood apart/was separated' (i.e. Dante's own position relative to the scene). The phrase sets the scene under that sky 'as I (was there/separated).'
segments.purgatorio-29-g91: ventiquattro seniori = 'twenty-four elders' (ventiquattro = 24; seniori = elders). The numeral and noun name the figures Dante sees.
segments.purgatorio-29-g92: a due a due is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'two by two' or 'in pairs' — the phrase literally 'at two, at two', indicating pairing.
segments.purgatorio-29-g93: coronati (past participle) = 'crowned' and agrees with seniori. venien is an archaic imperfect/pluperfect form of venire meaning 'they came' or 'were coming' used in narrative. di fiordaliso = 'with cornflower' (i.e. crowned with cornflowers); di indicates material or ornament.
segments.purgatorio-29-g94: Tutti = 'all/everyone'. cantavan is the imperfect (cantavano) 'they were singing' or 'they sang' in narrative style — a broad, ongoing past action.
segments.purgatorio-29-g95: Benedicta = feminine nominative 'blessed' (addressing the Virgin). tue = 'your'. ne le figlie d’Adamo = archaic use of ne = 'among/in' (here 'among the daughters of Adam'), le figlie = 'the daughters', d'Adamo = 'of Adam'. The line echoes liturgical language ('Blessed are you among women').
segments.purgatorio-29-g96: e = 'and'. benedette is feminine plural 'blessed' (agreeing with bellezze). sieno is subjunctive (archaic form of siano) 3rd person plural of essere: 'may they be' or 'that they be'. in etterno = 'in eternity' = 'forever'. le bellezze tue = 'your beauties' (subject of the subjunctive). Overall: an optative clause 'and may your beauties be blessed for ever.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g97: This segment contains only a period (punctuation) as a verse break; there are no words to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-29-g98: 'Poscia che' is a temporal conjunction meaning 'after'/'afterwards that' introducing the subordinate clause. 'I fiori e l’altre fresche erbette' is the compound subject (plural); 'a rimpetto di me da l’altra sponda' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'opposite me, on the other bank' (note the preposition sequence a + rimpetto + di me + da l’altra sponda). 'libere fuor da quelle genti elette' is the predicate: 'libere' (adjective) + the archaic/poetic 'fuor' (an archaic form used here in a past/result sense, roughly 'were out / were freed') and 'da quelle genti elette' ('from those chosen people').
segments.purgatorio-29-g99: 'sì come' introduces a simile ('just as'). The doubled 'luce luce' is a poetic repetition meaning 'light (one) after light (another)' or 'one light following another'; 'in ciel' = 'in heaven/sky'; 'seconda' is adjectival and here functions as 'following/second' (feminine to agree with luce).
segments.purgatorio-29-g100: 'Vennero' is the passato remoto (they came) of venire, used here narratively. 'appresso' is an adverb meaning 'after/close behind', and 'lor' = 'to/for them' (object pronoun); 'quattro animali' is the noun phrase that follows the verb because of Dante's poetic inversion (the animals are the ones who came).
segments.purgatorio-29-g101: 'coronati' is a past participle ('crowned') used predicatively and agreeing in gender/number with 'animali' (masculine plural). 'ciascun' is the distributive 'each' applied to the members of the plural subject (English often uses singular 'each', but here Dante uses the variant 'ciascun'); 'di verde fronda' is a prepositional phrase 'with/of green foliage' describing what the crowns were made of.
segments.purgatorio-29-g102: 'Ognuno' ('each one') is the subject. 'era' is the imperfect of essere ('was') used for descriptive past. 'pennuto' is an adjectival past participle meaning 'feathered' (used predicatively), and 'di sei ali' specifies the quantity 'with six wings'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g103: The clause 'le penne piene d’occhi' is a verbless descriptive phrase: 'the feathers full of eyes.' 'piene' is an adjective agreeing with 'penne' (feminine plural); 'd’occhi' ('of eyes') indicates what fills the feathers.
segments.purgatorio-29-g104: 'e' = 'and.' 'li occhi d’Argo' = 'the eyes of Argus' — a mythological reference (Argus Panoptes, the many‑eyed giant). The preposition 'd’' links possession ('of Argus').
segments.purgatorio-29-g105: 'se' introduces a conditional ('if'); 'fosser' is the archaic/subjunctive imperfect form of essere (modern 'fossero'), used here with 'se' to form the counterfactual 'if they were alive'; 'vivi' is the predicate adjective 'alive.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g106: 'sarebber' is the contracted conditional ('sarebbero' = 'would be') agreeing with the plural subject; 'cotali' = 'such'/'like these' (referring back to the described eyes/feathers). The whole clause says the eyes of Argus, if living, would be of that kind.
segments.purgatorio-29-g107: 'A descriver' (literally 'to describe') introduces the purpose/content; Dante uses the infinitive in a formulaic way ('As for describing...'). 'lor' = 'their' (poetic form of 'loro'). 'più non' = 'no longer', and 'spargo' is first-person present of 'spargere' ('I scatter/spread') — here meaning 'I no longer expend/compose rhymes for that purpose.' 'rime' is the direct object 'rhymes/verses.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g108: 'lettor' is a vocative, the shortened form of 'lettore' ('reader'), addressing the person hearing/reading the poem.
segments.purgatorio-29-g109: 'ch’' = 'che' ('for/that'); 'altra spesa' literally 'another undertaking/charge' — here meaning a different task or occupation; 'mi' is the indirect object 'to me' and 'strigne' (Dante's spelling) corresponds to 'stringe' — 'presses/constrains' (i.e., forces or compels me).
segments.purgatorio-29-g110: 'tanto ch’' = 'so that' (result clause). 'a questa non posso esser largo' = 'I cannot be expansive/loose about this' — 'posso' (I can) + 'esser largo' (be expansive/generous in treatment). The infinitive 'esser' follows the modal 'posso.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g111: 'ma' = 'but.' 'leggi' here is an imperative addressed to the reader: 'read (consult).' 'Ezechïel' names the prophet Ezekiel; Dante directs the reader to Ezekiel's description instead of further elaborating himself.
segments.purgatorio-29-g112: This relative clause 'che li dipigne...' modifies 'Ezechïel' (i.e., 'who depicts them'). 'dipigne' (old-form of 'dipinge') is present tense 'he depicts/paints.' 'come li vide venir' uses 'vide' + infinitive 'venir' (a common narrative construction meaning 'he saw them come'); 'da la fredda parte' = 'from the cold side/region'; the series 'con vento e con nube e con igne' lists the accompanying elements 'with wind and with cloud and with fire.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g113: 'e quali i troverai ne le sue carte' = 'and which you will find in his pages/writings.' 'troverai' is future second person singular ('you will find'); 'ne le sue carte' is an archaic way to say 'in his records/pages' ('ne' + prepositional phrase).
segments.purgatorio-29-g114: 'tali eran quivi' is an inverted descriptive statement: 'such were they there.' 'eran' is the imperfect plural 'were' (archaic form of 'erano'); 'quivi' = 'there.' The short clause summarizes that Ezekiel's depiction matches what the poet saw.
segments.purgatorio-29-g115: 'salvo ch’' = 'except that.' 'a le penne' = 'with respect to / concerning the feathers.' 'Giovanni è meco' = 'John is with me' (John agrees with the poet on that point), and 'e da lui si diparte' = 'and he departs from him' meaning John differs from Ezekiel on the feather detail; 'si diparte' is a reflexive verb meaning 'diverges/separates (in opinion).'
segments.purgatorio-29-g51: An adverbial phrase: 'Poco più' = 'a little more' + 'oltre' = 'beyond/ farther on'. Together they form the temporal/place introduction 'a little farther on.' Word order is typical for an opening adverbial in Dante.
segments.purgatorio-29-g52: Literal order: 'sette alberi d’oro falsava nel parere il lungo tratto...' — Dante places the phrase 'sette alberi d'oro' before the verb. 'falsava' is the imperfect of falsare ('to make appear false/ distort'), and 'nel parere' means 'to the appearance' (i.e., to the eye). 'il lungo tratto del mezzo' is the stretch of the middle space being affected, and the relative 'ch’era ancor tra noi e loro' specifies that this stretch still lay between us and them.
segments.purgatorio-29-g53: 'ma quand’ i’ fui sì presso di lor fatto,' — 'quand’ i’ fui' is the archaic/poetic form 'quando io fui' (when I was) using passato remoto 'fui' (I was/became). 'sì presso di lor fatto' literally 'made so near to them' — 'fatto' is a past participle used predicatively: the narrator describes his being brought/coming very near to them.
segments.purgatorio-29-g54: 'che l’obietto comun,' — 'che' is a relative pronoun linking to the preceding clause; 'l’obietto comun' = 'the common/ordinary object' (i.e. the usual visual object/trick). Note the article + noun 'l’ obietto' and the adjective 'comun' (common).
segments.purgatorio-29-g55: 'che ’l senso inganna,' — another relative clause: 'which deceives the sense(s)'. 'senso' here means the perceptual sense (sight), and 'inganna' is present indicative 'deceives' (Dante often uses present/past-tense verbs to state a general fact about perception).
segments.purgatorio-29-g56: 'non perdea per distanza alcun suo atto,' — 'non perdea' is the imperfect negative 'did not lose' (perdea = perdere). 'per distanza' = 'because of/by distance'; 'alcun suo atto' = 'any of its effect/act' (alcun here in negative context = 'any'). The clause says the trick did not lose any of its force from being far away until Dante approached.
segments.purgatorio-29-g57: 'la virtù ch’a ragion discorso ammanna,' — 'la virtù' = 'the faculty/power'; 'ch’' = 'which'; 'a ragion discorso' = 'to/for reasoned discourse' (a phrase describing the mental faculty of forming rational speech/judgment); 'ammanna' is an archaic/poetic verb here meaning 'urges/ prompts/ brings about' (the sense 'causes one to reason or speak'). Grammatically this clause names the power that enables Dante to form a reasoned judgment.
segments.purgatorio-29-g58: 'sì com’ elli eran candelabri apprese,' — 'sì com’' = 'just as/that'; 'elli' = 'they' (the seven golden trees); 'eran' = imperfect 'were'; 'candelabri' = 'candlesticks'; 'apprese' is the narrator's past tense 'I learned/perceived' (passato remoto 'appresi' spelled here 'apprese' in Dante). The subject 'I' is implied from earlier clauses: the faculty mentioned made him learn/perceive they were candlesticks.
segments.purgatorio-29-g59: 'e ne le voci del cantare ‘Osanna’.' — 'e' = 'and'; 'ne le voci' = 'in the voices' (ne = locative/partitive here, 'in/of them'); 'del cantare' = 'of the singing/chant'; 'Osanna' is the acclamation quoted. The phrase closes the clause by saying that in the voices of their singing Dante heard 'Osanna.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g60: Whole-line poetic phrasing: 'Di sopra' = 'above/above them'; verb 'fiammeggiava' (imperfect) = 'was blazing/gleaming'; 'il bello arnese' = 'the beautiful instrument/device' (arnese = tool/device, here a contrivance or shining apparatus). The comparative string 'più chiaro assai che luna per sereno di mezza notte nel suo mezzo mese' reads: 'much clearer than the moon on a clear (serene) mid-night in the middle of its month' — 'che' introduces the comparison and 'per sereno di mezza notte' is an idiomatic temporal/atmospheric phrase specifying a clear mid-night.
segments.purgatorio-29-g61: 'Io mi rivolsi' is the passato remoto (rivolsi) + reflexive 'mi' = 'I turned (myself)' or 'I turned my gaze'; 'd’ammirazion pieno' = 'full of admiration' (d' = di); 'al buon Virgilio' = indirect object 'to good Virgil.' The sequence shows subject + reflexive verb + complement + indirect object.
segments.purgatorio-29-g62: 'ed esso mi rispuose' — 'ed' = 'and'; 'esso' here = 'he' (Virgil); 'mi rispuose' is passato remoto of rispondere ('he replied to me'). 'con vista carca di stupor non meno' = 'with a gaze laden with no less astonishment' — 'carca' = 'loaded/full', 'non meno' = 'no less.' The phrase compares Virgil's astonishment to the narrator's.
segments.purgatorio-29-g63: 'Indi' = 'then/thence'; 'rendei' is passato remoto of rendere ('I returned/gave back'); 'l’aspetto' = 'the look/gaze'; 'a l’alte cose' = 'to the high/lofty things'; the relative clause 'che si movieno incontr’ a noi sì tardi' uses the imperfect 'movieno' = 'they were moving' with reflexive 'si' and 'incontr’ a noi' = 'toward us/ meeting us'; 'sì tardi' = 'so slowly/so late.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g64: 'che foran vinte da novelle spose.' — 'che' = 'which/that'; 'foran vinte' is an archaic past construction meaning 'they were overcome/vanquished' (foran = older poetic form related to 'furono'/'furon'); 'da' introduces the agent of the passive 'by'; 'novelle spose' = 'newly married brides' (novelle = young/new, spose = brides). The clause is a simile: they moved as though overcome by brides.
segments.purgatorio-29-g135: Subject fronting: 'Tre donne' is the plural subject. 'in giro' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'around' or 'in a circle'; 'da la destra rota' is a prepositional phrase giving the direction/source ('from the right wheel/turn'), with the article retained in Dante's older spelling. 'venian' is the imperfect indicative (3rd-person plural) meaning 'they were coming' or 'they came' in a continuous/past habitual sense; the gerund 'danzando' ('dancing') describes their manner ('coming while dancing').
segments.purgatorio-29-g136: ‘L’una’ = 'one (of them)'. 'tanto rossa' = 'so red'. The clause beginning with 'ch’' (che) is a result/degree clause: 'ch’a pena' = 'that scarcely/with difficulty'; 'fora' here is an archaic subjunctive/past-subjunctive form (Dante uses 'fora' where later Italian has 'fosse'), meaning 'would be / were', linking the subordinate clause to the main adjective, and 'dentro al foco' = 'inside the fire'; 'nota' is an adjectival past participle meaning 'noticeable/known'.
segments.purgatorio-29-g137: 'l’altr’ era' = 'the other was/seemed'. 'come se' introduces a hypothetical/comparative clause requiring the subjunctive. 'fossero state' is the (compound) imperfect/pluperfect subjunctive (literally 'had been') agreeing with the plural subject 'le carni e l'ossa' and yields the meaning 'as if (her) flesh and bones had been made of emerald.' 'di smeraldo fatte' places the material 'of emerald' before the past participle 'fatte' ('made'), a normal adjective-like construction.
segments.purgatorio-29-g138: 'la terza parea' = 'the third seemed/appeared'. 'neve' is used in simile as the noun 'snow' and 'testé' is an adverb meaning 'just now' or 'recently'. 'mossa' is an adjective/past participle meaning 'moved' or 'stirred', so the clause reads 'the third appeared like snow just stirred.' Note the inverted order 'parea neve' (verb + noun simile) for poetic effect.
segments.purgatorio-29-g139: 'e or parëan' = 'and now they seemed' (imperfect of 'parere' used descriptively). 'da la bianca tratte' is a passive/participial idea: Dante means they appeared 'drawn from' or 'derived from' the white (one). Here 'tratte' functions like a past participle 'drawn/derived' (feminine plural to agree), though the printed sense-list gives only noun senses; syntactically it qualifies the colour-source of their appearance.
segments.purgatorio-29-g140: Elliptical short clause: 'or da la rossa' = 'now from the red one' (i.e. now they seemed to be derived from the red). The preposition 'da' marks source and the feminine article/adjective agree with the previously mentioned feminine figures.
segments.purgatorio-29-g141: 'e dal canto di questa' = 'and by the singing/side of this one' — 'dal canto' can mean 'from the song' or 'by the chant' (here: her song/voice). 'l’altre' = 'the other women' (feminine plural). 'toglien l’andare' literally 'they take away the walking/pace' but idiomatically 'they set or determine the movement/pace'; 'e tarde e ratte' are two coordinated adjectives ('both slow and swift') modifying 'andare' and presenting a deliberate contrast.
segments.purgatorio-29-g142: 'Da la sinistra' = 'on/from the left' (locative). 'quattro facean festa' uses the imperfect 'facean' (Dantean form of 'facevano') meaning 'they were celebrating/they made a festival'; the verb is 3rd-person plural and the noun 'festa' is the direct object of the activity.
segments.purgatorio-29-g143: 'in porpore vestite' = 'clothed in purple.' 'in' introduces the material/colour; 'porpore' here refers to purple robes/vestments (Dante uses the plural form), and 'vestite' is a past participle functioning as an adjective (feminine plural) agreeing with the women.
segments.purgatorio-29-g144: 'dietro al modo d’una di lor' = 'following the manner/style of one of them.' 'dietro' here means 'after' or 'following'; 'al modo' = 'the manner'; the relative clause 'ch’avea tre occhi in testa' ('who had three eyes on her head') uses 'avea' (imperfect of 'avere') to describe a continuing attribute of that one woman.
segments.purgatorio-29-g145: Appresso is an adverb placed at the start meaning 'immediately after' or 'right after'. "tutto il pertrattato nodo" is a noun phrase in which pertrattato is a past participle used adjectivally ('the whole knot discussed/treated'); tutto intensifies 'the whole/entire'. Vidi is the first-person passato remoto of vedere ('I saw'), with due vecchi as its direct object. The phrase in abito dispari is a prepositional phrase modifying the men: abito = garment/habit and dispari = 'different/mismatched' (each wears a different habit).
segments.purgatorio-29-g146: Ma introduces a contrast ('but'). Pari agrees with the plural subject (the two old men) and here means 'equal' or 'alike' in some respect. In atto indicates 'in demeanour/gesture' (atto = manner/gesture), and the following e onesto e sodo lists qualities: onesto = honourable/decent, sodo = solid/firm/stout. The adjectives describe how, despite different dress, they were alike in bearing and respectable firmness.
segments.purgatorio-29-g147: L’un ('one of them') is the subject; si mostrava is the imperfect reflexive of mostrare meaning 'appeared' or 'showed himself'. Alcun de' famigliari functions as 'one of the household/attendants' (alcun here = 'one'/'a certain one'). The noun phrase di quel sommo Ipocràte is a genitive identifying whose attendant he is. The relative clause che natura a li animali fé... personifies Nature (natura) as agent: fé is passato remoto of fare ('made'), ell' = ella ('she'), and ha più cari = 'has (them) most dear' (= 'whom she loves most').
segments.purgatorio-29-g148: Mostrava l’altro = 'the other showed/displayed'; la contraria cura = 'the contrary care' or 'the opposite kind of treatment/duty' (contraria modifies cura). The phrase con una spada lucida e aguta is an accompaniment phrase ('with a gleaming and sharp sword'); lucida and aguta are feminine singular adjectives agreeing with spada and placed after it in typical poetic order.
segments.purgatorio-29-g149: Tal che introduces a result clause ('so that' / 'such that'). Di qua dal rio is an adverbial phrase meaning 'on this side of the stream/river' (qua = 'this side' here); dal = da + il 'from the'. Mi fé paura uses passato remoto of fare + noun paura to mean 'it made/gave me fear' = 'it frightened me', with mi the indirect object ('to me').
segments.purgatorio-29-g116: Lo spazio is the subject (Lo = the + spazio = space/area). Dentro a lor quattro is an adverbial phrase placed after the subject meaning “within/among the four of them” (lor = their/the four of them, here possessive). Contenne is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of contenere, so the clause order is subject — adverbial — verb — direct object (un carro = a chariot).
segments.purgatorio-29-g117: In su is a prepositional/adverbial combination meaning “on/upon” (poetic/archaic ordering), followed by the locator of the wheels: due rote = “two wheels.” The whole phrase modifies carro: the chariot is on two wheels.
segments.purgatorio-29-g118: Trïunfale is an adjective “triumphal/triumphant” modifying carro. The diaeresis on ï signals the syllable break (tri-ün-fale) in Dante's orthography; the adjective is postposed for poetic rhythm.
segments.purgatorio-29-g119: Ch’ = che is a relative pronoun “which/that.” Venire in venne (passato remoto 3rd sing.) is the main verb “came.” Tirato is a past participle used predicatively (“being pulled”), and the prepositional phrase al collo d’un grifon indicates the manner/place of attachment: “at/by the neck of a griffin.” Syntactically the relative clause describes the chariot (it came, having been pulled by the griffin at its neck).
segments.purgatorio-29-g120: Esso is the pronoun subject “it.” Tendeva is the imperfect tense (3rd sing.) of tendere, meaning “was stretching/spreading” (ongoing or repeated action). In sù = upwards; l’una e l’altra ale is an idiom meaning “the one wing and the other” = both wings. Tra introduces the location “between the mezzana and the three-and-three lists/bands” (poetic coordinated expression le tre e tre liste). Word order is subject — verb — directional phrase — object/locative.
segments.purgatorio-29-g121: Sì ch’ (sì che) introduces a result clause: “so that.” A + nulla here functions as a dative/indirect object phrase meaning “to no one” or “to nothing” (i.e., harmed no one). The result clause links the wing-motion to its harmlessness.
segments.purgatorio-29-g122: Fendendo is the gerund/ present participle of fendere, meaning “cleaving/cutting through.” It is circumstantial, describing an action simultaneous with the main verb of the result clause (“as it cleaved through”).
segments.purgatorio-29-g123: Facea is the imperfect of fare (3rd sing.), here meaning “did/caused.” Male is used as a noun meaning “harm” (not the adverb “badly”), so facea male = “caused harm” or “did harm.” Together with the preceding sì che a nulla, it yields “so that, cleaving, it did no harm to anyone.”
segments.purgatorio-29-g124: Tanto ... che is a result construction: Tanto salivan che non eran viste = “They rose so much/so high that they were not seen.” Salivan is imperfect (3rd pl.) of salire “they rose/climbed”; non eran viste uses erano + past participle viste in a passive/predicative way (“were not seen/visible”).
segments.purgatorio-29-g125: Le membra d’oro avea = “it had golden limbs.” Avea is imperfect (3rd sing.) of avere. Quant’ era uccello is an elliptical comparative clause: quant’ = quanto “as many as/as much as,” so the phrase means “as many as a bird has” (i.e. the number appropriate to a bird). The subject is understood from context (the creature described).
segments.purgatorio-29-g126: E bianche l’altre is elliptical for “and the others [were] white.” The copula/verb is omitted (understood from the previous clause), and bianche (feminine plural) is a predicative adjective agreeing with l’altre.
segments.purgatorio-29-g127: Di vermiglio miste uses di to indicate material/colour (“mixed with vermilion”). Miste is the past participle/adjective “mixed,” agreeing feminine plural with le altre; di vermiglio specifies the mixing substance/colour.
segments.purgatorio-29-g128: Non che introduces a clarifying/contrasting clause: “It is not that ...” Rallegrasse is in the imperfect subjunctive form (–sse), used here in the clause after non che to express the hypothetical/resultative idea “would have gladdened.” Roma di carro così bello: di carro qualifies Roma as possessing such a chariot. Affricano = “the African” (personal epithet).
segments.purgatorio-29-g129: O vero Augusto is an appended alternative/appellative: o = “or/oh,” vero = “truly/rightly,” Augusto = “Augustus” (addressed by name). Grammatically this names another figure who would not be gladdened either.
segments.purgatorio-29-g130: Ma introduces contrast “but.” Quel del Sol = “that one of the Sun” (i.e. the Sun’s chariot). Saria is the conditional (archaic/simplified form of sarebbe) meaning “would be.” Pover = “poor” (predicative adjective). Con ello = “compared with it/with that,” where con marks comparison and ello is the neuter pronoun “it.”
segments.purgatorio-29-g131: Quel del Sol che begins a relative clause: “that of the Sun, which ...” Che here is the relative pronoun introducing the clause that follows and refers back to 'quel del Sol.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g132: Svïando (sviare) is a gerund/present participle meaning “straying/diverting.” The diaeresis marks Dante’s pronunciation; here it describes the manner in which the Sun’s chariot acted — “having strayed” or “in straying.”
segments.purgatorio-29-g133: Fu combusto is a passive perfect (passive past) in passato remoto: “was burned/was consumed.” Per introduces cause/agent here: per l’orazion de la Terra devota = “by/through the prayer of the devout Earth” (Terra personified). Orazion is a noun “prayer,” and devota is an adjective qualifying Terra.
segments.purgatorio-29-g134: Quando introduces a temporal clause: “when.” Fu is the past historic of essere (“was”). Giove is the proper name Jupiter; arcanamente is an adverb “secretly/arcane-ly” modifying giusto, which here means “just/right” — together meaning “when Jupiter was secretly just” (i.e., acted in some hidden justice).
segments.purgatorio-29-g150: Poi is an adverb meaning 'then' introducing the next action. Vidi is the first-person past (passato remoto) of vidi/vido, 'I saw'. The phrase in umile paruta is descriptive: in + adjective + past participle (paruta, a poetic/archaic past participle meaning 'appeared' or 'seemed') gives the sense 'appearing in humble guise.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g151: This clause continues the object of vidi: Italian allows a perception verb + noun phrase + infinitive, so un vecchio solo venir is 'an old man alone coming' as seen by the speaker. Di retro da tutti is a stacked prepositional expression meaning 'from behind all (of them)' with di + retro ('from/behind') followed by da tutti ('from all').
segments.purgatorio-29-g152: Dormendo is a present participle / gerund of dormire, here functioning as a circumstantial participle 'sleeping' that modifies the old man; it indicates simultaneous action.
segments.purgatorio-29-g153: Con + noun is a simple prepositional phrase 'with.' La faccia is the noun phrase 'the face/expression' (faccia + definite article). Arguta is an adjective (feminine) agreeing with faccia and here means 'sharp' or 'keen' rather than just 'witty' — a brief description of his expression.
segments.purgatorio-29-g154: E 'and' links this sentence to the previous one. Col is the contracted form of con + il ('with the'), so col primaio stuolo = 'with the first troop.' Erano abitüati is the imperfect construction (erano + past participle) meaning 'were accustomed' — a past habitual/state description.
segments.purgatorio-29-g155: Ma begins a contrast ('but'). Di gigli modifies the following phrase: 'of lilies.' Dintorno al capo is an adverbial expression 'around the head.' Non facëan is an archaic imperfect form of fare ('they did/made') with the tréma on ë to show pronunciation; here 'they did not make.' Brolo is an older noun meaning an enclosed garden/garland, so non facëan brolo means 'they did not make garlands.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g156: Anzi here functions 'rather' or 'instead': Dante corrects the preceding expectation. The sequence di rose e d’altri fior vermigli lists the actual flowers: di and d’ are the same preposition 'of,' and vermigli is an adjective (plural) meaning 'vermilion/ crimson,' agreeing with fior.
segments.purgatorio-29-g157: This is an impersonal, inverted conditional/periphrastic expression: giurato avria literally places the past participle before the conditional auxiliary (avria = 'would have'), a poetic inversion meaning 'one would have sworn.' Poco lontano aspetto = 'at a short distance (by appearance/at first glance)'; aspetto here means 'appearance' or 'look.' Che introduces the subordinate clause 'that all ardesser...': ardesser is an archaic third-person plural imperfect form of ardere ('to burn'), meaning 'they were burning.' Di sopra da' cigli = 'from above the eyelashes/brows' (da + cigli).
segments.purgatorio-29-g158: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when.' Il carro is the subject ('the chariot'); fu is the passato remoto of essere ('was'). A me is a dative/locative 'to me' (i.e., in my sight), and a rimpetto means 'opposite' or 'in front of.' The whole clause = 'when the chariot was opposite me.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g159: Un tuon 'a thunder' is the subject of the small clause; s’udì is udì (past of udire 'to hear') with the clitic si producing an impersonal/passive sense — 'was heard' or 'one heard' — so the combined phrase means 'a thunder was heard.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g160: E quelle genti degne 'and those worthy people' is the subject phrase. Parvero is an archaic past form meaning 'seemed' (from parere). Aver l’andar is an idiom ('to have the walk/gait') meaning 'to carry oneself in a certain way'; più interdetto modifies it: interdetto here is figurative, meaning 'more checked, dazed, or disconcerted' rather than literally 'forbidden.' The whole clause means 'seemed to have a more constrained/halting gait.'
segments.purgatorio-29-g161: Fermandosi is a reflexive gerund/participle from fermare, 'halting/stopping themselves,' indicating a simultaneous or resultant action. Ivi is an adverb 'there.' Con le prime insegne is a prepositional phrase 'with the first banners/emblems'; insegne is plural of insegna ('banner, emblem').

sentences.purgatorio-29-s9: And a sweet melody ran through the luminous air; from it a good zeal made me reproach Eve’s boldness — who, there where earth and heaven obey, a woman, alone and just now formed, would not endure to remain beneath any veil; under which, had she been devout, I would have perceived those ineffable delights earlier and for a longer time.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s10: While I was going among so many first-fruits of eternal pleasure, all suspended, and still desirous of yet greater delights, before us, just like a kindled fire, the air made itself for us beneath the green boughs; and the sweet sound from the songs was already heard.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s11: O sacrosanct Virgins, if I have ever endured hunger, cold or vigils for you, the reason urges me that I call upon your mercy.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s12: Now it is fitting that Helicon compose verses for me, and that the Uranias aid me with their chorus to put weighty matters — hard to think — into verse.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s1: Singing like a woman in love, she continued to the end of her words: "Blessed are they whose sins are covered!"
sentences.purgatorio-29-s3: And like nymphs who went alone through the wild shades, some longing to see, some to flee the sun, then she moved toward the river, going up along the bank; and I, matching her, followed with small steps.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s4: There were not a hundred between her steps and mine, when the banks equally made a turn, so that I found myself facing east.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s5: Nor had our path yet been so long when the woman completely turned to me, saying, "My brother, look and listen."
sentences.purgatorio-29-s6: And behold, a sudden gleam ran from every direction through the great forest, so that its flashing put me in doubt.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s7: But because the flash, as it comes, remains, and that one, continuing, shone more and more, in my thought I was saying: 'What is this?'
sentences.purgatorio-29-s17: The lady scolded me: "Why do you still burn so in the feeling for the living lights, and do you not look at what comes behind them?"
sentences.purgatorio-29-s19: Then I saw people, as their leaders, come up behind them, dressed in white; and such a whiteness had never before been seen here.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s20: The water pressed in on the left flank, and my left bank showed me my left side, if I looked into it, like a mirror as well.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s21: When I had taken up that position on my bank, so that only the river made me distant, I stopped to see the steps better, and I saw little flames going ahead, leaving behind them the air painted, and having the appearance of brushstrokes; so that there above remained, distinct, seven bands, all in those colors with which the Sun makes the arch and Delia the girdle.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s22: Those things shown behind were larger than my sight (could take); and, as far as my judgment went, the ones outside were ten paces distant.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s23: Under so fair a sky as I was (there), twenty-four elders, two by two, came crowned with cornflowers.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s24: They all sang: 'Blessed are you among the daughters of Adam, and may your beauties be blessed in eternity!'
sentences.purgatorio-29-s26: After the flowers and the other fresh little herbs opposite me on the far bank were freed from those chosen people, just as light follows light in heaven, four animals came after them, each crowned with green foliage.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s27: Each was feathered with six wings; the feathers were full of eyes; and the eyes of Argus, if he were alive, would be like these.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s28: I no longer spread rhymes to describe their forms, reader; for another task presses me, so that I cannot be expansive on this; but read Ezekiel, who depicts them as he saw them coming from the cold side with wind and with cloud and with fire; and which you will find in his pages — such were they there, except that regarding the feathers John is with me and differs from him.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s13: A little farther on, seven golden trees made the long stretch of the middle space that still lay between us and them appear deceptive to the eye; but when I had come so close to them that the ordinary visual trick which deceives the senses lost none of its effect through distance, the faculty that prompts reasoned judgment taught me what they were — that they were candlesticks — and I heard 'Hosanna' in the voices of their singing.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s14: Above it the beautiful device blazed, far clearer than the moon on a serene night about the middle of its month.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s15: I turned, full of admiration, to good Virgil, and he answered me with a look no less full of astonishment.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s16: Then I turned my gaze back to the lofty things that were moving to meet us so slowly that they seemed to have been overcome by newly-wedded brides.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s33: Three women were circling from the right wheel, coming while dancing; one so red that she would scarcely be recognizable inside the fire; another seemed as if her flesh and bones had been made of emerald; the third appeared like snow just stirred; and now they seemed drawn from the white, now from the red; and by the singing of this one the others take their pace, both slow and swift.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s34: On the left four were making a festival, dressed in purple, following the manner of one of them who had three eyes on her head.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s35: Right after the knot I had just described, I saw two old men in mismatched dress,
sentences.purgatorio-29-s36: One of them seemed to be one of the attendants of that great Hippocrates—whom Nature had made for the animals she loves most; the other displayed the contrary care, with a bright, sharp sword, so that, on this side of the stream, it frightened me.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s29: The space among the four of them contained a triumphal chariot on two wheels, which came being pulled by a griffin by the neck.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s30: It spread both wings upward between the middle one and the three-and-three bands, so that, as it cleaved (the air), it did no harm to anyone.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s31: They rose so high that they were not seen; it had golden limbs as many as a bird has, and the others were white, mixed with vermilion.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s32: Not that Rome’s chariot of such beauty would have gladdened the African, or indeed Augustus; but the Sun’s would have seemed poor beside it — the Sun’s chariot which, straying, was burned up by the prayer of the devout Earth, when Jupiter was secretly just.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s37: Then I saw four, appearing in humble guise; and from behind them all an old man alone came, sleeping, with a keen face.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s38: And these seven were accustomed with the first troop, but they did not make garlands of lilies around their heads; rather they had roses and other crimson flowers; one would have sworn, at a short distance by their appearance, that all were burning above the eyelids.
sentences.purgatorio-29-s39: And when the chariot was opposite me, a thunderclap was heard, and those worthy people seemed to carry a more constrained gait, halting there with the first banners.
